<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:三角縁神獣鏡]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:三角縁神獣鏡]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:三角縁神獣鏡]]

{{Expand Japanese|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}}

'''Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror''' (三角縁神獣鏡, Sankakuen Shinjūkyō<ref name="kunaicho">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://www.kunaicho.go.jp/culture/shoryobu/syuzou-r05.html |title=Details of Collection / Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror - Imperial Household Agency |website=www.kunaicho.go.jp |accessdate=2016-09-15}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/52345 |title=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror Cultural Heritage Online |website=bunka.nii.ac.jp |accessdate=2016-09-15}}</ref>, also pronounced さんかくぶちしんじゅうきょう, Sankakubuchi Shinjūkyō<ref>{{Cite encyclopedia |title=Divine Beast Mirror |encyclopedia=National History Dictionary |publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan}}</ref><ref>{{Cite encyclopedia |title=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror |encyclopedia=[[Nipponica Encyclopedia|日本大百科全書 (Nipponica)]] |publisher=Shogakukan}}</ref>) is a type of {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}} characterized by its triangular cross-sectional rim and classified as a large-scale [[Shinju-kyo|divine beast mirror]].
== Overview ==

Copper mirrors have been excavated from archaeological sites spanning from the middle of the Yayoi period to Kofun-period tombs, with over 4,000 mirrors unearthed so far<ref>{{Cite journal |author=Hisanobu Nishikawa |year=1999 |title=Triangular-rimmed mirrors and Himiko's Mirrors |journal=Japanese Archaeology |volume=6 |issue=8 |pages=87-99 |doi=10.11215/nihonkokogaku1994.6.8_87}}</ref>. Among these, mirrors classified as triangular-rimmed mirrors with mythical beast designs exceed 330 examples{{疑問点|date=2018-10-23|talksection=On the total number and distribution of mirrors}} and are the most commonly excavated type of mirror in Japan<ref>{{Cite Kotobank |word=三角縁神獣鏡 |encyclopedia=[[Encyclopedia Britannica]] Small Entry |accessdate=2018-10-15}}</ref>, although precise information on their excavation contexts and distribution remains scarce.

These mirrors are predominantly unearthed from early Kofun-period tombs in Japan, averaging approximately 20 cm in diameter. Their designs feature deities and mythical creatures cast on the back of the mirror, and two examples bear inscriptions that include the year names of the Chinese Wei Dynasty.

The triangular rims are believed to have been adopted for structural ease, as most of these mirrors are convex, or to imitate the divine enclosures of sacred places. In China, triangular-rimmed mirrors without mythical beast designs have been found near {{ill|Shaoxing|en|Shaoxing|WD=Q42666|lt=Shaoxing|1=Shaoxing}} and date to the 2nd and 3rd centuries. However, there are no records of triangular-rimmed mythical beast mirrors being found on the Korean Peninsula.

== Features of Triangular-Rimmed Mirrors ==

=== Definitions ===

Defining triangular-rimmed mirrors with mythical beast designs is challenging, but Takayasu Higuchi identifies the following six criteria for classification<ref>{{Cite book |author=Takayasu Higuchi |year=1992 |title=Triangular-Rimmed Mythical Beast Mirrors: A Comprehensive Study |publisher=[[Shinchosha]] |pp=243-244}}</ref>:

* Large size, often exceeding 20 cm in diameter.
* Triangular cross-section of the rim.
* The outer zone features sawtooth, double-line wave, and sawtooth patterns.
* Inner bands include motifs such as inscriptions, beast designs, arabesques, wave patterns, and semicircular forms.
* The central area is divided into four to six sections by conical or hemispherical knobs, with deities and auspicious beasts arranged concentrically or in the same direction.
* Inscriptions in seven-character or four-character phrases.

Mirrors that meet these criteria are classified as imported or of Chinese manufacture<ref>{{Cite book |author=Hitoshi Shitagaki |year=2010 |title=Encyclopedia of Triangular-Rimmed Mythical Beast Mirrors |publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kobunkan]] |pp=11-14}}</ref>.

=== Ornamentation ===

The motifs on the back of the mirrors often depict divine realms but are not always rendered with a deep understanding of their symbolism. The central areas may feature immortal figures, animals such as dragons or tigers, and auspicious items like parasols and censer designs<ref>{{Cite book |author=Hitoshi Shitagaki |year=2010 |pp=20-28}}</ref>.

== Significant Kofun with Triangular-Rimmed Mirrors ==
{{Miscellaneous content bullet list|section=1|date=April 2021}}
The distribution of triangular-rimmed mirrors with deities and beasts by prefecture shows that the highest number is found in Nara Prefecture with 100 mirrors, followed by Kyoto Prefecture with 66, Hyōgo Prefecture with over 40, and Osaka Prefecture with 38. The mirrors are predominantly excavated in the Kinki region, centered on the former {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}}, but they are distributed nationwide from Kyushu to Tohoku.

|-

| {{ill|Higashi Tomezuka Kofun|ja|東求女塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Tomezuka Kofun|qq=}} || Hyōgo Prefecture, Kobe, Higashinada Ward || Keyhole-shaped mound || Early Period || Four mirrors excavated
|-
| {{ill|Koyadani Kofun|ja|コヤダニ古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koyadani Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Hyōgo Prefecture, {{ill|Sumoto, Hyōgo|en|Sumoto, Hyōgo|WD=Q369628|lt=Sumoto, Hyōgo|1=Sumoto, Hyōgo}} || || ||
|-
| {{ill|Yoshima Kofun|en|Yoshima Kofun|WD=Q38279346|lt=Yoshijima Kofun|1=Yoshima Kofun}} || Hyōgo Prefecture, {{ill|Tatsuno, Hyōgo|en|Tatsuno, Hyōgo|WD=Q877190|lt=Tatsuno, Hyōgo|1=Tatsuno, Hyōgo}} || Keyhole-shaped mound || || Four mirrors excavated out of six; same mold as Tsubai Ōtsukayama Mound
|-
| {{ill|Morio Kofun|ja|森尾古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Morio Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Hyōgo Prefecture, {{ill|Toyooka, Hyōgo|en|Toyooka, Hyōgo|WD=Q696388|lt=Toyooka, Hyōgo|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}, Morio, Ichio || || || Mirror inscribed "Zhengshi First Year"
|-
! colspan=5| Chūgoku Region
|-
| {{ill|Bizen Kurazuka Kofun|ja|備前車塚古墳|ja|備前車塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bizen Kurazuka Kofun|qq=}} || Okayama Prefecture, {{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|WD=Q200078|lt=Okayama|1=Okayama}} || Keyhole-shaped mound || || 11 mirrors excavated out of 13
|-
| {{ill|Tsuruyama Maruyama Kofun|en|Tsuruyama Maruyama Kofun|WD=Q117038368|lt=Tsuruoyama Maruyama Kofun|1=Tsuruyama Maruyama Kofun}} || Okayama Prefecture, {{ill|Bizen, Okayama|en|Bizen, Okayama|WD=Q863741|lt=Bizen, Okayama|1=Bizen, Okayama}} || Round mound || || One mirror excavated out of 30
|-
| {{ill|Shiratori Kofun (disambiguation)|lt=Shiratori Kofun (disambiguation)|lt=Shiratori Kofun|ja|白鳥古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiratori Kofun|qq=}} || Hiroshima Prefecture, {{ill|Higashihiroshima|en|Higashihiroshima|WD=Q653372|lt=Higashihiroshima|1=Higashihiroshima}}, Takaya Town || || ||
|-
| {{ill|Shiozaki Kofun|ja|潮崎山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiozaki Kofun|qq=}} || Hiroshima Prefecture, {{ill|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|en|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|WD=Q661038|lt=Fukuyama|1=Fukuyama, Hiroshima}}, Shinichi Town || || ||
|-
| {{ill|Nakakoda No. 1 Kofun|ja|中小田1号墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakakoda No. 1 Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Hiroshima Prefecture, {{ill|Hiroshima|en|Hiroshima|WD=Q34664|lt=Hiroshima|1=Hiroshima}}, Higashi Ward || || || Same mold as Tsubai Ōtsukayama Mound
|-
| {{ill|Kanbara Shrine|lt=Kanbara Shrine|lt=Kambara Shrine Kofun|ja|神原神社古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kambara Shrine Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神原神社}} || Shimane Prefecture, {{ill|Unnan|en|Unnan|WD=Q877935|lt=Unnan, Shimane|1=Unnan}}, Kamo Town || || || Mirror inscribed "Jingchu Third Year"
|-
| {{ill|Takeshima Go-Karō Residence Kofun|ja|竹島御家老屋敷古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeshima Go-Karō Residence Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Yamaguchi Prefecture, {{ill|Shūnan|en|Shūnan|WD=Q643929|lt=Shūnan, Yamaguchi|1=Shūnan}} || || || Mirror inscribed "Zhengshi First Year"
|-
! colspan=5| Shikoku Region
|-
| {{ill|Oku No. 3 Kofun|ja|奥三号墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku No. 3 Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Kagawa Prefecture, {{ill|Sanuki, Kagawa|en|Sanuki, Kagawa|WD=Q857202|lt=Sanuki, Kagawa|1=Sanuki, Kagawa}}, Samukawa Town || Keyhole-shaped mound || Early Kofun Period ||
|-
| {{ill|Nishiyama Kofun|ja|西山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishiyama Kofun|qq=}} || Kagawa Prefecture, Sanuki, Kamobe Nishiyama || Keyhole-shaped mound || Early Kofun Period ||
|-
| {{ill|Miyatani Kofun|ja|宮谷古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyatani Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Tokushima Prefecture, {{ill|Tokushima (city)|en|Tokushima (city)|WD=Q207309|lt=Tokushima|1=Tokushima (city)}}, Kokufu Town || Keyhole-shaped mound || Late 3rd to early 4th century ||
|-
! colspan=5| Kyushu Region
|-
| {{ill|Naka Hachiman Kofun|ja|那珂八幡古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naka Hachiman Kofun|qq=}} || Fukuoka Prefecture, {{ill|Fukuoka|en|Fukuoka|WD=Q26600|lt=Fukuoka|1=Fukuoka}}, Hakata Ward || Keyhole-shaped mound || Mid-3rd century || Five-God Four-Beast Mirror
|-
| {{ill|Ishizukayama Kofun|en|Ishizukayama Kofun|WD=Q11585466|lt=Ishizukayama Kofun|1=Ishizukayama Kofun}} || Fukuoka Prefecture, {{ill|Miyako District, Fukuoka|en|Miyako District, Fukuoka|WD=Q1156359|lt=Miyako District, Fukuoka|1=Miyako District, Fukuoka}}, {{ill|Kanda, Fukuoka|en|Kanda, Fukuoka|WD=Q1206876|lt=Kanda, Fukuoka|1=Kanda, Fukuoka}} || Keyhole-shaped mound || Late 3rd century || Same mold as Tsubai Ōtsukayama Mound; seven mirrors excavated
|-
| {{ill|Ikisan Chōshizuka Kofun|en|Ikisan Chōshizuka Kofun|WD=Q48752705|lt=Ikisan Chōshizuka Kofun|1=Ikisan Chōshizuka Kofun}} || Fukuoka Prefecture, {{ill|Itoshima, Fukuoka|en|Itoshima, Fukuoka|WD=Q1011145|lt=Itoshima, Fukuoka|1=Itoshima}} || Keyhole-shaped mound || Late 4th century || Eight locally produced mirrors excavated
|-
| {{ill|Iwagami Ritual Site|ja|岩上祭祀遺構|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwagami Ritual Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || Fukuoka Prefecture, {{ill|Munakata, Fukuoka|en|Munakata, Fukuoka|WD=Q819603|lt=Munakata, Fukuoka|1=Munakata, Fukuoka}}, Okinoshima || || Late 4th to early 5th century || Ancient ritual site, not a Kofun
|-
| {{ill|Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster|lt=Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster|lt=Akasuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 川部・高森古墳群}} || Ōita Prefecture, {{ill|Usa, Ōita|en|Usa, Ōita|WD=Q114147|lt=Usa, Ōita|1=Usa, Ōita}}, Usa Fudoki Hill || Keyhole-shaped mound || Late 3rd century || Part of {{ill|Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster|en|Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11479328|lt=Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster|1=Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster}}; Kyushu's oldest Kofun; five mirrors excavated
|-
| {{ill|Mochida Kofun Cluster|en|Mochida Kofun Cluster|WD=Q107346207|lt=Mochida Kofuns|1=Mochida Kofun Cluster}} || Miyazaki Prefecture, {{ill|Koyu District, Miyazaki|en|Koyu District, Miyazaki|WD=Q1132169|lt=Koyu District, Miyazaki|1=Koyu District, Miyazaki}}, {{ill|Takanabe, Miyazaki|en|Takanabe, Miyazaki|WD=Q1206737|lt=Takanabe, Miyazaki|1=Takanabe, Miyazaki}}, Mochida || || || Mirror inscribed "Heavenly King Sun and Moon." Same mold as Tsubai Ōtsukayama Mound<ref name="miyazaki">{{Cite journal |和書  |author=Noriyuki Imashioya |year=2010 |title=The Formation and Evaluation of the Mochida Kofuns in Takanabe Town, Miyazaki Prefecture |journal=Miyazaki Cultural Studies Research Bulletin |issue=37 |publisher=Miyazaki Prefectural Library |pages=47-65 |crid=1522543655217461888 |url=https://www.lib.pref.miyazaki.lg.jp/ct/other000001600/kiyouimashioya.pdf}}</ref><ref>{{Cite Kotobank |word=Mochida Kofuns |encyclopedia=Nipponica Encyclopedia |accessdate=2018-10-23}}</ref>
|-
| Mochida Old Mound No. 48 || Miyazaki Prefecture, Koyu District, Takanabe Town, Mochida || Round mound || || Part of Mochida Kofuns. Same mold as Maebashi Tenjinyama Mound<ref name="miyazaki" />. Extensively looted in the early Shōwa period<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.miyazaki-archive.jp/d-museum/mch/details/view/1849|title=Cultural Properties of Miyazaki: Mochida Kofuns |accessdate=2018-10-23}}</ref>.
|}
== Hypothesis Linking to Himiko ==

Theories suggest a connection between triangular-rimmed mythical beast mirrors and the "100 bronze mirrors" reportedly bestowed by the Wei Dynasty to Himiko, a queen of [[Yamatai-koku]]<ref>{{Cite journal |author=Toshio Fukuyama |year=1987 |title=Himiko's Mirrors |journal=Japanese Archaeology |pp=121}}</ref>. While some researchers link these mirrors to Himiko’s era, others argue that they postdate her reign and were locally manufactured.
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}}
* {{ill|Shinju-kyo|en|Shinju-kyo|WD=Q7497614|lt=Shinju-kyo|1=Shinju-kyo}}
* {{ill|Chinese magic mirror|en|Chinese magic mirror|WD=Q2919513|lt=Chinese magic mirror|1=Chinese magic mirror}}
* {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|WD=Q844295|lt=Yamatai|1=Yamatai}}      

==References==
<references />
[[File:蟹沢古墳出土 三角縁同向式神獣鏡.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Triangular Rim, Same-direction Divine Beast Mirror|ja|三角縁同向式神獣鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular Rim, Same-direction Divine Beast Mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} “□始元年” ({{ill|Shoshigen-nen|ja|正始元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoshigen-nen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) inscription – Excavated from the {{ill|Kanazawa†Kanazawa_City|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanazawa†Kanazawa_City|qq=}} site of {{ill|Kani Sawako Kofun|ja|蟹沢古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kani Sawako Kofun|qq=}} in {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}}, held by the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}, designated as an Important Cultural Property]]
[[File:Mirror with Triangular Rim and Design of Buddhist Characters and Animals, Kofun period, 4th century, from Shinyama Kofun, Koryo-cho, Nara - Tokyo National Museum - DSC06346.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{ill|Triangular Rim, Three Gods Three Beasts Mirror|ja|三角縁三神三獣鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular Rim, Three Gods Three Beasts Mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} – Excavated from the {{ill|Shinyama Kofun|ja|新山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinyama Kofun|qq=}} in Hiro ({{ill|Hiroshima†Hiroshima_Prefecture|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiroshima†Hiroshima_Prefecture|qq=}} region) and held in the collection of the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} (displayed at the Tokyo National Museum)]]

'''Triangular Rim Divine Beast Mirror''' ({{ill|Triangular Rim Divine Beast Mirror|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular Rim Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}}, also read as さんかくえんしんじゅうきょう or さんかくぶちしんじゅうきょう) is a type of {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}} characterized by a rim whose cross‐section is triangular. It belongs to the larger category of {{ill|Shinju-kyo|en|Shinju-kyo|WD=Q7497614|lt=divine beast mirrors|1=Shinju-kyo}}, and in this variant the design along the rim is modified into a triangular shape.

== Overview ==
Bronze mirrors have been excavated in Japan from sites dating from the mid-{{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|WD=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}} to Kofuns of the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}, with roughly over 4,000 examples reported<ref>{{Cite journal |和書|author=西川寿勝 |authorlink=西川寿勝 |year=1999 |title=三角縁神獣鏡と卑弥呼の鏡 |journal=日本考古学 |volume=6 |issue=8 |pages=87-99 |doi=10.11215/nihonkokogaku1994.6.8_87}}</ref>. Among these, the bronze mirrors classified as triangular rim divine beast mirrors exceed 330 in number{{疑問点|date=2018-10-23|talksection=鏡の総数とその分布について}} (note: one source citing “over 540” lacks proper citation) – making them the most numerous type among mirrors excavated in Japan<ref>{{Cite Kotobank |word=三角縁神獣鏡 |encyclopedia={{ill|Britannica International Encyclopedia|ja|ブリタニカ国際大百科事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Britannica International Encyclopedia|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} 小項目事典 |accessdate=2018-10-15}}</ref>. However, few comprehensive studies accurately document their excavation conditions and distribution.

These mirrors, which appear frequently from the early {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}} in Japan, typically have an average diameter of about 20 centimeters. On the mirror back ({{ill|mirror reverse|ja|鏡背|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mirror reverse|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), images of divine beasts ({{ill|divine beast|ja|神獣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/divine beast|qq=}}) are cast. Two examples bearing inscriptions with a Chinese era name from the state of {{ill|Wei (Three Kingdoms)|ja|魏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wei (Three Kingdoms)|qq=}} have been excavated.

The reasons for the triangular form of the rim are debated. One explanation is that because most bronze mirrors are {{ill|convex mirrors|ja|凸面鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/convex mirrors|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, a triangular rim is structurally easier to manufacture; another theory suggests it was intended to mimic a sacred barrier ({{ill|mizugaki|ja|瑞垣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mizugaki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 玉垣}}) surrounding a holy place. In China, however, non-divine-beast bronze mirrors with triangular rims have only been found from the 2nd to 3rd centuries near {{ill|Shaoxing|ja|紹興|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shaoxing|qq=}}, and no such mirrors have been discovered on the Korean Peninsula.

Prior to the appearance of triangular rim divine beast mirrors in Japan, during the early 3rd century, Han-style mirrors known as {{ill|Pictorial Divine Beast Mirrors with Inscribed Bands|ja|画文帯神獣鏡|lt=wide image-band deity and beast mirror|1=Shinju-kyo|2=ja|3=神獣鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pictorial Divine Beast Mirrors with Inscribed Bands|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} were excavated primarily in the Kinai region. Some scholars argue that the designs of these pictorial mirrors were cleverly modified and mass-produced domestically in Japan as a reinterpretation of the imagery seen on imported pictorial mirrors.

Research on triangular rim divine beast mirrors has expanded rapidly since the 1990s, leading to extensive debate and numerous conflicting interpretations. Many views have been linked with discussions about {{ill|Himiko|en|Himiko|WD=Q234451|lt=Himiko|1=Himiko}} and the ancient {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|WD=Q844295|lt=Yamatai State|1=Yamatai}}, sometimes in an overly simplistic and sensationalist manner that has even drawn in mass media attention<ref>{{sfn|下垣仁志|2010|pp=262-266}}</ref>. Consequently, there are many differing opinions even on basic matters, and consensus has not yet been reached.

== Characteristics of Triangular Rim Divine Beast Mirrors ==
=== Definition ===
[[File:三角縁神獣鏡の部分名称.png|thumb|350px|Labelled parts of a Triangular Rim Divine Beast Mirror]]
Although it is difficult to define precisely what constitutes a triangular rim divine beast mirror, {{ill|Takayasu Higuchi|ja|樋口隆康|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayasu Higuchi|qq=}} proposed that a mirror must meet the following six criteria<ref>{{sfn|樋口隆康|1992|pp=243-244}}</ref>:
* It is usually a large mirror with a diameter exceeding 20 centimeters.
* Its rim cross-section is triangular.
* The outer zone consists of decorative bands of serrated ({{ill|sawtooth pattern|ja|鋸歯文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sawtooth pattern|qq=}}), double-lined wavy ({{ill|double wavy pattern|ja|複線波文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/double wavy pattern|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), and again serrated motifs.
* Along the outer circumference of the inner zone, one of the following bands is arranged: an inscription band, a beast band, a arabesque band, a wavy band, a sawtooth band, a pictorial band, a bead band, or a semicircular square band.
* The main pictorial area (主文区) of the inner zone is evenly subdivided by 4 to 6 small conical or hemispherical projections (乳), between which images of deities and auspicious beasts are arranged either uniformly in the same direction ({{ill|same-direction type|ja|同向式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/same-direction type|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) or oriented toward the center ({{ill|centripetal type|ja|求心式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/centripetal type|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
* The inscriptions on the inscription band include several phrases: several seven-character and one four-character formula.
Mirrors satisfying these conditions are regarded as being of Chinese origin or as imported, according to some scholars<ref>{{sfn|下垣仁志|2010|pp=11-14}}</ref>.

On the other hand, there are mirrors considered to be imitations ({{ill|faux copies|ja|仿製鏡|1=Bōseikyō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/faux copies|13=User:Immanuelle/Bōseikyō|qq=}}) produced in the Japanese archipelago. {{ill|Kenzo Tomioka|ja|富岡謙蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenzo Tomioka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} provided the following four criteria to differentiate these:
* The decorative patterns are vague and sometimes overly simplified or rendered meaningless.
* The motifs have lost their original meaning.
* The inscriptions are deficient or treated in a symbolic manner.
* Bells are attached along the rim.
Mirrors meeting these criteria are excluded from the classification of triangular rim divine beast mirrors. As of 2010, about 430 Chinese-made examples and about 130 imitations have been counted<ref>{{sfn|下垣仁志|2010|pp=11-14}}</ref>, making them extraordinarily numerous among the artifacts of the period, if one excludes everyday items such as pottery<ref>{{sfn|下垣仁志|2010|pp=1-8}}</ref>. However, note that differing definitions lead to different counts; for example, {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koichi Mori|qq=}} criticizes the inclusion of a mirror depicting a dragon and tiger (龍虎鏡) as a triangular rim divine beast mirror<ref>{{sfn|森浩一|2015|pp=208-212}}</ref>.

The name "triangular rim divine beast mirror" became widely accepted after {{ill|Mamihara Sueharu|ja|梅原末治|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mamihara Sueharu|qq=}} adopted that term following an earlier designation by {{ill|Mori Mamoru|ja|後藤守一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori Mamoru|qq=}} as "triangular rim style divine beast mirror"<ref>{{sfn|下垣仁志|2010|p=94}}</ref>.

=== Features of the Mirror Back ===
The decorative patterns on the mirror back are said to represent the realm of immortals, though some designs do not appear to reflect a deep understanding of that mythology<ref>{{sfn|下垣仁志|2010|p=30}}</ref>. Triangular rim divine beast mirrors are named based on the motifs in the outer circumference of the inner zone and the inner zone itself. For example, if an inscription band contains an inscription beginning with "吾作" and the inner zone features four divine images and two auspicious beast images arranged in the same direction, the mirror is referred to as a "(triangular rim) Wosaku Four Gods Two Beasts Mirror"<ref>{{sfn|下垣仁志|2010|pp=14-15}}</ref>.
; Outer Zone
: The basic structure consists of three bands – a sawtooth pattern band, a double-lined wavy pattern band, and another sawtooth pattern band. In some cases, the double-lined wavy pattern is replaced by a single-lined wavy pattern or arranged diagonally. Some mirrors also feature a protruding line (突線) along the outermost circumference; the presence or absence of this feature has been suggested to indicate lineage.
: The inscriptions are predominantly in four- or seven-character phrases, with some mirrors featuring shorter repetitive phrases such as "天王日月" or "君冝高官" within a square. According to one classification by {{ill|Takayasu Higuchi|ja|樋口隆康|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayasu Higuchi|qq=}}, the inscriptions can be broadly categorized into 20 types and more finely into 27 types. Although the content of the inscriptions concerns deities of immortality, they are unique to these divine beast mirrors and have been criticized as having “crude phonetics” or being “patchworked,” which some proponents of a domestic origin cite as evidence for a Japanese-made mirror; however, other researchers point out that vocabulary characteristic of Wei–Jin mirrors is also evident<ref>{{sfn|下垣仁志|2010|pp=28-29}}</ref>. In imitations, the inscriptions sometimes show reversed characters on the left and right, suggesting that they were produced by Japanese craftsmen lacking knowledge of Chinese characters<ref>{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2020|pp=155-158}}</ref>.
; Boundary Band
: Around the outer edge of both the inner zone and its boundary is a boundary band (界圏) with a cross-section in the shape of a right triangle. The outer face of this band is often undecorated, while the inner face is typically adorned with a sawtooth pattern. In some cases, the boundary band is absent and is replaced by a mere border line (圏線)<ref>{{sfn|下垣仁志|2010|pp=15-17}}</ref>.
; Inner Zone
: The inner zone is evenly subdivided by 4 to 6 small projections (乳), with each segment filled with decorative motifs such as deities of immortality, auspicious beasts, attendant warriors, chariots and horses, and umbrellas or pine trees. The arrangement of the images is broadly categorized into "same-direction type" (where all images face upward) and "centripetal type" (where images are oriented toward the center), and can be further subdivided into more than 20 types. Variations exist in the shape of the projections and their bases<ref>{{sfn|下垣仁志|2010|pp=17-19}}</ref>.
: The deities depicted in the inner zone are often indeterminate, though candidates such as {{ill|Queen Mother of the West|en|Queen Mother of the West|WD=Q1064059|lt=Xiwangmu|1=Queen Mother of the West}}, {{ill|King Father of the East|en|King Father of the East|WD=Q326677|lt=Dongwangfu|1=King Father of the East}}, Wang Qiao (王子喬), and Akamatsu-ko have been suggested. Xiwangmu, derived from the {{ill|Classic of Mountains and Seas|en|Classic of Mountains and Seas|WD=Q844278|lt=Classic of Mountains and Seas|1=Classic of Mountains and Seas}}, was originally considered to be androgynous before being separated into Dongwangfu; these two deities are frequently used in mirrors from the latter half of the Later Han period, where they played a role in balancing yin and yang. Xiwangmu and Dongwangfu have been identified in inscriptions<ref>{{sfn|下垣仁志|2010|pp=20-25}}</ref>. Wang Qiao and Akamatsu-ko are representative immortals. In other divine beast mirrors, figures identified as attendant deities for Xiwangmu or Dongwangfu have been depicted, and it is suggested that in some triangular rim divine beast mirrors the two attendant deities might be Wang Qiao and Akamatsu-ko, although the inscriptions do not name them<ref>{{sfn|下垣仁志|2010|pp=20-25}}</ref>. Additionally, there is a theory that figures depicted in a [[Seated in Lotus Position|meditative lotus posture]] with the {{ill|Zen seal|ja|禅定印|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zen seal|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and with a topknot are interpreted as Buddhist images. Among the auspicious beast motifs, there are images of a running beast with an open mouth and a "pan-dragon" in which the body is hidden within a buckle while the head and limbs protrude. Inscriptions reveal a variety of names including "dragon", "Qinglong", "tiger", "{{ill|White Tiger (mythology)|en|White Tiger (mythology)|WD=Q1072455|lt=White Tiger|1=White Tiger (mythology)}}", "heavenly deer", "vermillion bird", "{{ill|Black Turtle-Snake|en|Black Turtle-Snake|WD=Q125696|lt=Xuanwu|1=Black Turtle-Snake}}", and "Shin-mori" (guardian deity)<ref>{{sfn|下垣仁志|2010|pp=25-26}}</ref>. Other motifs include the "Kasa-matsu" (umbrella pine) pattern and the "Hakusan-ro" (incense burner shaped like Mount Hakusan). The Kasa-matsu motif (located at the 8 o’clock position in the partial diagram of a triangular rim divine beast mirror) was once interpreted as "Kihata", but later theories based on the inscription suggest it represents "Setsu" (a vessel borne by an envoy), and some researchers even argue that it is a uniquely Japanese motif that supports a domestic origin.
; Knobs
: Knobs (鈕) are generally undecorated, though their cross-sectional shape is used for classification. There is, however, one example of a knob in the shape of a toad (蛙). Knobs have holes (鈕孔) through which cords pass, and these holes are most often rectangular in cross-section—a feature not found in mirrors from Wu, according to some researchers. In some cases, the knob holes show no evidence of polishing, or remnants of unburned clay (used in casting) remain, which has been cited as evidence that these were nonfunctional ceremonial objects<ref>{{sfn|下垣仁志|2010|pp=19-20}}</ref>.

=== Other Characteristics ===
* They have been excavated from a wide area centered on the {{ill|Kinai|en|Kinai|WD=Q906708|lt=Kinai region|1=Kinai}}, and are predominantly found in Kofuns dating from the early {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}.
* Many mirrors were produced using the same mold ({{ill|dōhōkyō|ja|同笵鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/dōhōkyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
* Although early theories posited that they were of Chinese manufacture, no such examples have been found in China.

Research on triangular rim divine beast mirrors has the potential to illuminate various issues, including:
* The logistics of the Kofun period
* The political context of ancient state formation
* The actual chronology of kofun construction
* The chronology of the introduction of Buddhism
* Interactions with ancient East Asian societies
* Theories of Chinese production techniques and ideologies
Of particular note is the suggested connection with {{ill|Himiko|en|Himiko|WD=Q234451|lt=Himiko|1=Himiko}}, which has attracted considerable attention<ref>{{sfn|下垣仁志|2010|pp=1-8}}</ref>.

== Main Research ==
This section describes the history of research on triangular rim divine beast mirrors. (The dates in parentheses indicate the approximate years when these theories were proposed.)
{{Note: The following is a summary of major research studies:}}

{{!-- 
Example of summaries:
- Kenzo Tomioka (1920) proposed that triangular rim divine beast mirrors are Wei mirrors, and subsequently, in conjunction with the Yamataikoku-Kinki theory, they came to be associated with Himiko's mirror.
- Yukio Kobayashi (1976) reinforced this view through the so-called "dōhō mirror theory" and "densetsu mirror theory," advancing the classification of imported mirrors and imitations.
- At the same time, studies by Mamoru Nishida (1972) and Kōichi Kondō (1973) demonstrated that the lineage of imported mirrors can be traced to Chinese divine beast mirrors, and that imitation mirrors represent a new stage of the imported mirror.
- From as early as 1962, criticism of the Chinese-origin theory was raised by Hirokazu Mori, and later, in 2020, a report from China regarding the excavation of a triangular rim divine beast mirror in Luoyang was found to be ambiguous, leaving the Chinese-origin hypothesis unsubstantiated.
- Conversely, Takashi Tanaka (1985) proposed a "special casting theory" for mirrors destined for the Japanese archipelago, given the lack of Chinese examples.
- Other scholars, such as Nakajima (1980s), have proposed that immigrants from Wu produced these mirrors domestically in Japan, based on the concentration of divine beast mirrors in the Jiangnan region.
- Critics such as Masao Okuno (1982) have examined past excavation data and noted that certain motifs (e.g., the "Kasa-matsu" design) found on these mirrors are absent from Chinese counterparts, arguing that these mirrors are crudely made imitations produced in Japan.
- In 1986, a mirror bearing an inscription with the non-existent era name “Keishoshi-nen” was excavated, complicating the issue further. Kōichi Kondō (1988) later suggested that this might be due to a misprint or that the mirror was not made in Luoyang.
- In the 1990s, research began focusing on production techniques. Shinya Fukunaga (1991) argued that the characteristic placement of rectangular knob holes at a single level in triangular rim divine beast mirrors is limited to mirrors from the 3rd century, and that the outer protruding lines (突線) observed are a feature seen in Wei–Jin mirrors along the eastern part of North China and the Bohai Bay.
- Around the same time, studies of Wei mirrors progressed, and Fukuyoshi Fukunaga and Shōji Morishita (2000) pointed out that the inscriptions on Wei–Jin mirrors (the Fanggeikijiku mirrors) and those on triangular rim divine beast mirrors excavated from the Matsubayama Kofun are identical, while Ueno Shōshi (2000) demonstrated that divine beast mirrors were produced in eastern North China.
- Others, such as Masahiko Kurazaki (1999), argued from the decorative motifs that even the imitation mirrors are Chinese-made, with imported mirrors being Wei mirrors and imitations belonging to the Western Jin period.
- Additionally, Kōji Shimizu (2015) observed similar mold marks on both imported and imitation mirrors, suggesting they were produced by the same craftsmen and using the same mold, thereby arguing that they are either all Chinese-made or all Japanese-made.
- Since 1989, type-based classification and chronology have advanced, with models proposing 3 to 5 stages of change; however, two main theories regarding production period exist: one by Kurazaki (1999) suggesting a short production period of about 20 years (with imported mirrors identified as Wei mirrors) and another by Morishita (1998) and Fukunaga (1994) suggesting a longer period of about 50 years, correlating the production period with the changing chronology of kofun construction.
- Since 1980, scientific studies have also progressed. Hisao Mabuchi and Yoshimitsu Hirao (1982) used lead isotope analysis to demonstrate that the lead in mirrors from the former Han and later Han periods came from different sources, and that the bronze mirrors made in Japan share the lead source with later Han mirrors. Although triangular rim divine beast mirrors show some commonalities with later Han mirrors in a broad sense, variability in the data has led to the proposal of both Chinese-made and Japanese-made theories. The Izumiya Hakko Museum (2004) suggested, based on microelement analysis using SPRING8, that they may have been produced using the same raw materials as Wei–Jin mirrors. Other studies have noted differences in the data between imported and imitation mirrors, but sample sizes are small and consensus on production location remains elusive. Moreover, even if the raw material source is the same as the production location, that does not necessarily prove identical origins<ref>{{sfn|辻田淳一郎|2020|pp=159-160}}</ref>.
--}}

== Himiko Mirror Theory ==
Regarding the controversy over the location of the {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|WD=Q844295|lt=Yamatai State|1=Yamatai}}, various theories abound, and it is thought that definitive physical evidence—such as Himiko’s tomb, the golden seal bestowed by Wei to the "親魏倭王" (King of Wa who submitted to Wei), or a set of 100 bronze mirrors—could settle the debate. Some researchers, particularly those supporting the Kinki-centric Himiko theory<ref group="Note">Based on the concentration of triangular rim divine beast mirrors in the Kinki region</ref>, argue that Himiko’s mirror is of the triangular rim divine beast mirror type.

The evidence cited in support of this theory includes:
* An inscription recording the Wei era name of {{ill|Jingchu|ja|景初|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingchu|qq=}} 3 (239 AD) sent by Himiko’s envoy<ref>{{sfn|福山敏男|1987|pp=121}}</ref>
* An inscription stating "Bronze originates from {{ill|Xuzhou (ancient China)|en|Xuzhou (ancient China)|WD=Q17025191|lt=Xuzhou|1=Xuzhou (ancient China)}}, and the craftsmen come from {{ill|Luoyang|ja|洛陽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Luoyang|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 洛陽市}}" <ref>{{sfn|富岡謙蔵|1916|pp=120}}</ref>
* Similarities in characteristics with Wei mirrors<ref>{{sfn|田中琢|1985|p=60}}</ref>
* Elemental analysis showing lead composition similar to Wei mirrors<ref>{{sfn|馬淵久夫|1996}}</ref>

Conversely, opponents argue that the mirrors are domestically produced, are newer than Himiko’s time, or lack high artistic value, citing:
* Not a single example has been found in China<ref>{{sfn|王仲殊|1981|pp=346-358}}</ref>
* An inscription with the non-existent era name "景初四年" (supposedly 240 AD) <ref>{{sfn|王仲殊|1987|pp=265-271}}</ref>
* The number of mirrors far exceeds 100<ref>{{sfn|古田武彦|2016|pp=262-263}}</ref>
* Elemental analysis suggests the copper ore may have originated from {{ill|Kamio Copper Mine|ja|神尾銅山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamio Copper Mine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
<ref>{{sfn|新井宏|2007}}</ref>
* The excavation context of the {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|en|Kurozuka Kofun|WD=Q1793171|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|1=Kurozuka Kofun}} indicates that triangular rim divine beast mirrors were treated roughly<ref>{{sfn|森浩一|1978|pp=51-95}}</ref>
* Many of the mirrors date to the 4th century<ref>{{sfn|森浩一|2015|pp=204-207}}</ref>
* The inscriptions are of a crude quality<ref>{{sfn|森博達|2003|pp=32-39}}</ref>
* There are features shared with mirrors from {{ill|吳|en|吳|WD=Q96621022|lt=Wu|1=吳}}<ref>{{sfn|王仲殊|1981|pp=346-358}}</ref>

These conflicting lines of evidence have led to ongoing debate with no definitive conclusion.

== Chronologically Inscribed Triangular Rim Divine Beast Mirrors ==
{{Referencesの明記|date=July 2020|section=1}}
Among the triangular rim divine beast mirrors, there are four examples bearing inscriptions with Wei era names. These include:
; The “{{ill|Jingchu 3|ja|景初三年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingchu 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” mirror (239 AD)
: Inscription (partially reconstructed): “In Jingchu 3, Chen Shizuo made this mirror …” (text partially obscured)
; The “{{ill|Zhengshi 1|ja|正始元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhengshi 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” mirror (240 AD)
: Inscription (partially reconstructed): “In Zhengshi 1, Chen Shizuo made this mirror …”
(For reference, below are examples of chronologically inscribed mirrors other than triangular rim divine beast mirrors:)
; “{{ill|Jingchu 4|ja|景初四年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingchu 4|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” mirror (240 AD)
: Inscription: “In Jingchu 4, on the day of May Bingwu, Chen Shizuo made this mirror …” – a mirror from the [[Hiragami 15-go Kofun]] (a slanted-rimmed pan-dragon mirror)
: (Since Jingchu appears to end at 3, this remains controversial.)
; “{{ill|Qinglong 3|ja|青龍三年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qinglong 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” mirror (235 AD)
: Inscription: “In Qinglong 3, ...” – a mirror from the [[Anmangu Yama Kofun]] (a patterned divine beast mirror)

== On the Same-Mold (Dōhō) Mirrors ==
A notable characteristic of triangular rim divine beast mirrors is the large number of mirrors with virtually identical decorative motifs that have been produced and sometimes excavated from geographically distant sites. This phenomenon has spurred debate in the context of ancient state politics and economic distribution.

Several theories have been proposed regarding the method of producing a group of mirrors from the same mold:
* **Same-mold method (同笵法)** – repeatedly using a single mold (笵) for casting.
* **Identical-type method (同型法)** – creating a master model from an original mold, from which multiple molds are copied; the molds are then discarded after use.
* **Wax-model method (同型法（蝋型）)** – producing multiple wax models from an original mold, and then copying several molds from the wax models; both the wax models and the molds are discarded.
* **Progressive copying method (踏み返し法)** – creating a master model from an original mold, then copying a second mold from the master, then creating a second master from the second mold, and so on; in other words, the finished mirror is used as the model for subsequent copies.
Mirrors produced by these methods are sometimes given different designations based on their casting method; for convenience in this article, they are uniformly referred to as {{ill|Same-Mold Mirrors|ja|同笵鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Same-Mold Mirrors|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref>{{sfn|下垣仁志|2010|pp=30-34}}</ref>.

Research on the manufacturing method of same-mold mirrors has explored issues such as the progressive accumulation of mold damage (笵傷), the tendency for finished mirrors to be smaller than the original due to metal contraction during casting, and the warping of the mirror surface due to such contraction, as well as various experimental reproductions. However, no definitive theory has yet been established<ref>{{sfn|下垣仁志|2010|pp=217-227}}</ref>.

One example of same-mold mirror research is summarized in the table below, which is based on data from the "Catalog of Same-Mold Divine Beast Mirrors," "List of Excavation Sites of Same-Mold Mirrors," "Illustrated Diagram of Same-Mold Mirror Types," and "Inscriptions on Triangular Rim Divine Beast Mirrors" from the Triangular Rim Divine Beast Mirror Research Encyclopedia<ref>{{sfn|下垣仁志|2010|pp=417-535}}</ref>.

{| class="wikitable"
|+ Same-Mold Mirrors Excavated from the {{ill|Mannen-yama Kofun|ja|万年山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mannen-yama Kofun|qq=}}
! Same-Mold Mirror Number<ref group="Note">Based on the same-mold mirror numbering diagram created by {{ill|Yukio Kobayashi|ja|小林行雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yukio Kobayashi|qq=}} in 1985; those not listed in that diagram are marked with an asterisk.</ref>
! Mirror Name<ref group="Note">Following Kobayashi’s naming convention, though the “Dragon-Tiger Mirror” has been re-designated as a pan-dragon mirror.</ref>
! Inscription<ref group="Note">Indeterminate characters are marked with an asterisk.</ref>
! Diameter (cm)
! Excavation Site of the Same-Mold Mirror<ref group="Note">Listed in the order of prefecture name then excavation site.</ref>
! Mirror Diameter (cm)
! Special Notes
|-
| rowspan="3" | 3
| rowspan="3" | {{ill|Haniwa-band Pan-Dragon Mirror|ja|波文帯盤龍鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haniwa-band Pan-Dragon Mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
| rowspan="3" | 
| rowspan="3" | 22.0
| {{ill|Hyōgo†Hy%C5%8Dgo_Prefecture|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgo†Hy%C5%8Dgo_Prefecture|qq=}} – {{ill|Yoshima Kofun|en|Yoshima Kofun|WD=Q38279346|lt=Yoshijima Kofun|1=Yoshima Kofun}}
| 22.3
| 
|-
| {{ill|Nara†Nara_Prefecture|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara†Nara_Prefecture|qq=}} – {{ill|Ikenochi No. 5 Kofun|ja|池ノ内5号墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikenochi No. 5 Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
| 22.1
| 
|-
| {{ill|Gunma†Gunma_Prefecture|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gunma†Gunma_Prefecture|qq=}} – {{ill|Yorimoto Kofun|ja|頼母子古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yorimoto Kofun|qq=}}
| 21.7
| 
|-
| rowspan="5" | 19
| rowspan="5" | {{ill|Wosaku Four Gods Four Beasts Mirror|ja|吾作四神四獣鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wosaku Four Gods Four Beasts Mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
| rowspan="5" | “Wosaku Myōkyō Jin Daikō East<br>Above is Wang Qiao with Akamatsu East<br>Master Lion, Heavenly Deer, its 粦, and Dragon East<br>Under heaven, renowned, peerless East”
| rowspan="5" | 19.9
| {{ill|Fukuoka†Fukuoka_Prefecture|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuoka†Fukuoka_Prefecture|qq=}} – {{ill|Bushu Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|豊前石塚山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bushu Ishizukayama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
| 19.9
| Designated as an Important Cultural Property (excavated from the Bushū Ishizukayama Kofun in Fukuoka)
|-
| {{ill|Hiroshima†Hiroshima_Prefecture|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiroshima†Hiroshima_Prefecture|qq=}} – {{ill|Nakashoda No. 1 Kofun|ja|中小田1号墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakashoda No. 1 Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
| 20.1
| 
|-
| {{ill|Hyōgo†Hy%C5%8Dgo_Prefecture|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōgo†Hy%C5%8Dgo_Prefecture|qq=}} – {{ill|Nishimotomezuka Kofun|en|Nishimotomezuka Kofun|WD=Q11628958|lt=Nishi Kyujozuka Kofun|1=Nishimotomezuka Kofun}}
| 19.9
| Designated as an Important Cultural Property (excavated from the Nishi Kyujozuka Kofun in Hyōgo)
|-
| {{ill|Kyoto†Kyoto_Prefecture|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto†Kyoto_Prefecture|qq=}} – {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|en|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|WD=Q11540994|lt=Tsubinai Ōtsukayama Kofun|1=Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun}}
| 19.8
| Designated as an Important Cultural Property (excavated from the Tsubinai Ōtsukayama Kofun in Kyoto); mirror found in two pieces
|-
| {{ill|Nara†Nara_Prefecture|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara†Nara_Prefecture|qq=}} – {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|en|Kurozuka Kofun|WD=Q1793171|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|1=Kurozuka Kofun}}
| 20.0
| Designated as an Important Cultural Property (excavated from the Kurotsuka Kofun in Nara)
|-
| *
| {{ill|Chen Shizuo Six Gods Four Beasts Mirror|ja|陳是作六神四獣鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chen Shizuo Six Gods Four Beasts Mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
| “Chen Shizuo Jin Daikō Great Good – Mysterious<br>Above are Gidori and Dragon, with a fish<br>On the body, inscriptions like Giant Fish<br>Ancient sages, King of the East, fish<br>Drought-drinking, jade mortar, hunger-eating date mysterious”
| 22.0
| {{ill|Fukuoka†Fukuoka_Prefecture|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuoka†Fukuoka_Prefecture|qq=}} – {{ill|Myōhōji No. 2 Kofun|ja|妙法寺2号墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōhōji No. 2 Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
| 21.9
| 
|-
| rowspan="1" | 41
| {{ill|Nichigetsu Nichijitsu·Tangcao-banda Four Gods Four Beasts Mirror|ja|日月日日・唐草文帯四神四獣鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nichigetsu Nichijitsu·Tangcao-banda Four Gods Four Beasts Mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
| “Nichigetsu Nichijitsu”
| 21.9
| {{ill|Shizuoka†Shizuoka_Prefecture|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shizuoka†Shizuoka_Prefecture|qq=}} – {{ill|Kyozuka Kofun|ja|経塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyozuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 経塚古墳 (笛吹市)}}
| 22.0
| 
|-
| rowspan="1" | 64

| “Kimi Gen Tak＊”
| 22.1
| {{ill|Nara†Nara_Prefecture|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara†Nara_Prefecture|qq=}} – {{ill|Samida Takarazuka Kofun|en|Samida Takarazuka Kofun|WD=Q11383079|lt=Samida Takazuka Kofun|1=Samida Takarazuka Kofun}}

| 23.3
| {{ill|Kagawa†Kagawa_Prefecture|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagawa†Kagawa_Prefecture|qq=}} – {{ill|Hasajichausuya Kofun|ja|蓮尺茶臼山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasajichausuya Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
| 23.2
| 
|}
== Images ==
<gallery caption="Triangular Rim Divine Beast Mirrors">
File:会津大塚山古墳南棺出土 三角縁唐草文帯三神二獣鏡.JPG|{{ill|Triangular Rim, Four Gods Two Beasts Mirror|ja|三角縁三神二獣鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular Rim, Four Gods Two Beasts Mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<br/>{{small|Excavated from the 【ill|Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|会津大塚山古墳】 in Aizuwakamatsu, Fukushima Prefecture; held by the Fukushima Prefectural Museum (Important Cultural Property).}}
File:前橋天神山古墳出土 三角縁四神四獣鏡.JPG|{{ill|Triangular Rim, Four Gods Four Beasts Mirror|ja|三角縁四神四獣鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular Rim, Four Gods Four Beasts Mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<br/>{{small|Excavated from the 【ill|Maebashi Tenjinsan Kofun|ja|前橋天神山古墳】 in Maebashi, Gunma Prefecture; held by the Tokyo National Museum (Important Cultural Property).}}
File:前橋天神山古墳出土 三角縁五神四獣鏡.JPG|{{ill|Triangular Rim, Five Gods Four Beasts Mirror|ja|三角縁五神四獣鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular Rim, Five Gods Four Beasts Mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<br/>{{small|Excavated from the Maebashi Tenjinsan Kofun in Maebashi, Gunma Prefecture; held by the Tokyo National Museum (Important Cultural Property).}}
File:城山1号墳 (香取市) 出土 三角縁三神五獣鏡 (複製).JPG|{{ill|Triangular Rim, Three Gods Five Beasts Mirror|ja|三角縁三神五獣鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular Rim, Three Gods Five Beasts Mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (replica)<br/>{{small|Excavated from the 【ill|Jōyama No. 1 Kofun (in Katori City)|ja|城山1号墳 (香取市)】 in Katori, Chiba Prefecture; held by the Katori City Cultural Property Museum.}}
File:真土大塚山古墳出土 三角縁四神二獣鏡.JPG|{{ill|Triangular Rim, Four Gods Two Beasts Mirror|ja|三角縁四神二獣鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular Rim, Four Gods Two Beasts Mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<br/>{{small|Excavated from the 【ill|Matsudo Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|真土大塚山古墳】 in Hiratsuka, Kanagawa Prefecture; held by the Tokyo National Museum.}}
File:Mirror with Triangular Rim and Dragon and Tiger Design, 12 of 12, Japan, Kofun period, 200s-300s AD, bronze - Tokyo National Museum - Tokyo, Japan - DSC09187.jpg|{{ill|Triangular Rim, Three Gods Three Beasts Mirror|ja|三角縁三神三獣鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular Rim, Three Gods Three Beasts Mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<br/>{{small|Excavated from a site in 【ill|Sakurai|ja|桜井市】, Nara Prefecture; held by the Tokyo National Museum.}}
File:松林山古墳出土 三角縁吾作銘二神二獣鏡.JPG|{{ill|Triangular Rim, Wosaku Inscription, Two Gods Two Beasts Mirror|ja|三角縁吾作銘二神二獣鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular Rim, Wosaku Inscription, Two Gods Two Beasts Mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<br/>{{small|Excavated from the 【ill|Matsubayama Kofun|ja|松林山古墳】 in Iwata, Shizuoka Prefecture; held by the Tokyo National Museum.}}
File:Bronze Mirror in Ancient Japan.jpg|{{ill|Triangular Rim, Four Gods Four Beasts Mirror|ja|三角縁四神四獣鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular Rim, Four Gods Four Beasts Mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (replica)<br/>{{small|Excavated from the 【ill|Akamon-ue Kofun|ja|赤門上古墳】 in Hamamatsu, Shizuoka Prefecture.}}
File:久津川車塚古墳出土 三角縁四神四獣鏡.JPG|{{ill|Triangular Rim, Four Gods Four Beasts Mirror|ja|三角縁四神四獣鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular Rim, Four Gods Four Beasts Mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<br/>{{small|Excavated from the 【ill|Kutsukagawa Kuramatsuzuka Kofun|ja|久津川車塚古墳】 in Jōyō, Kyoto Prefecture; held by the Izumiya Hakko Museum (Important Cultural Property).}}
File:Mirror with Triangular Rim and Dragon and Tiger Design, 11 of 12, Japan, Kofun period, 200s-300s AD, bronze - Tokyo National Museum - Tokyo, Japan - DSC09185.jpg|{{ill|Triangular Rim, Two Gods Two Beasts Mirror|ja|三角縁二神二獣鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular Rim, Two Gods Two Beasts Mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<br/>{{small|Excavation location unspecified; held by the Tokyo National Museum.}}
File:出土地不詳 大槻磐渓旧蔵 三角縁三神三獣鏡.JPG|{{ill|Triangular Rim, Three Gods Three Beasts Mirror|ja|三角縁三神三獣鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular Rim, Three Gods Three Beasts Mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<br/>{{small|Excavation location unspecified; held by the Kokugakuin University Museum.}}
</gallery>

<gallery caption="Others">
File:甲斐銚子塚古墳出土 三角縁神獣車馬鏡.JPG|{{ill|Triangular Rim, Divine Beast Charioteer Mirror|ja|三角縁神獣車馬鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular Rim, Divine Beast Charioteer Mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<br/>{{small|Excavated from the 【ill|Kai Choushizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳】 in Kofu, Yamanashi Prefecture; held by the Tokyo National Museum.}}
File:Mirror with Triangular Rim and Dragon and Tiger Design, 5 of 12, Japan, Kofun period, 200s-300s AD, bronze - Tokyo National Museum - Tokyo, Japan - DSC09172.jpg|{{ill|Triangular Rim, Divine Beast Charioteer Mirror|ja|三角縁神獣車馬鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular Rim, Divine Beast Charioteer Mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<br/>{{small|Excavated from the 【ill|Ōiwayama Kofun|ja|大岩山古墳】 in Yasushi, Shiga Prefecture; held by the Tokyo National Museum.}}
File:和泉黄金塚古墳出土 三角縁龍虎鏡.JPG|{{ill|Triangular Rim, Dragon-Tiger Mirror|ja|三角縁龍虎鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular Rim, Dragon-Tiger Mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<br/>{{small|Excavated from the 【ill|Izumi Ōgonzuka Kofun|ja|和泉黄金塚古墳】 in Izumi, Osaka Prefecture; held by the Tokyo National Museum (Important Cultural Property).}}
File:富雄丸山古墳出土 三角縁龍虎鏡.JPG|{{ill|Triangular Rim, Dragon-Tiger Mirror|ja|三角縁龍虎鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular Rim, Dragon-Tiger Mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<br/>{{small|Excavated from the 【ill|Tomio Maruyama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳】 in Nara City, Nara Prefecture; held by the Tenri Reference Center.}}
File:雪野山古墳出土 三角縁波文帯盤龍鏡 (3号鏡).JPG|{{ill|Triangular Rim, Haniwa-band Pan-Dragon Mirror|ja|三角縁盤龍鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular Rim, Haniwa-band Pan-Dragon Mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<br/>{{small|Excavated from the 【ill|Yukino-yama Kofun|ja|雪野山古墳】 in Higashi-Kinomi, Shiga Prefecture; held by the Higashi-Kinomi Municipal Cultural Property Center (Important Cultural Property).}}
</gallery>

== Main Kofun Confirmed to Yield Triangular Rim Divine Beast Mirrors ==
{{雑多な内容の箇条書き|section=1|date=April 2021}}
Based on available ({{Citation needed|date=2018-10}}) data on the distribution of triangular rim divine beast mirrors by prefecture, the majority are found in the Kinki region: starting with over 100 from {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}, 66 from {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}, over 40 from {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, and 38 from {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. However, they have been excavated nationwide from Kyushu to the Tohoku region. (For example, in Fukuoka Prefecture more than 40, and in the former {{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|WD=Q1155128|lt=Kibi State|1=Kingdom of Kibi}} over 28 have been reported. This suggests that the Kinki region, especially Nara, Kyoto, and Osaka, accounts for more than half of the total mirrors excavated, exceeding 270 in number. Detailed references for these distribution numbers are lacking; see notes.)

{| class="wikitable" style="font-size:smaller; width:100%;"
|+ Detailed Distribution
|-
! Kofun Name !! Location !! Shape !! Period !! Features
|-
! colspan=5 | Tohoku Region
|-
| {{ill|Ōtsukayama Kofun|en|Ōtsukayama Kofun|WD=Q11381233|lt=Aizu Ōtsukayama Kofun|1=Ōtsukayama Kofun}}
|| {{ill|Aizuwakamatsu|en|Aizuwakamatsu|WD=Q237699|lt=Aizuwakamatsu City|1=Aizuwakamatsu}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|en|Fukushima Prefecture|WD=Q71707|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|1=Fukushima Prefecture}} || || 4th century || Among the oldest in the Tohoku region; designated a National Historic Site in 1972 (Showa 47)
|-
! colspan=5 | Kanto Region
|-
| {{ill|Maebashi Tenjinsan Kofun|ja|前橋天神山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maebashi Tenjinsan Kofun|qq=}}
|| {{ill|Maebashi|en|Maebashi|WD=Q201613|lt=Maebashi City|1=Maebashi}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}} || {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} || Late 4th century || Two mirrors of the Four Gods Four Beasts type were excavated
|-
| {{ill|Yorimoto Kofun|ja|頼母子古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yorimoto Kofun|qq=}} (extinct)
|| {{ill|Ōta, Gunma|en|Ōta, Gunma|WD=Q386179|lt=Ota City|1=Ōta, Gunma}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}} || Uncertain (but likely keyhole-shaped) || Mid-4th century || Two triangular rim divine beast mirrors were found (one now missing)
|-
| {{ill|Kitayama Chausuyama Kofun|ja|北山茶臼山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitayama Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}}
|| {{ill|Tomioka, Gunma|en|Tomioka, Gunma|WD=Q873835|lt=Tomioka City|1=Tomioka, Gunma}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}} || Circular (possibly keyhole-shaped) || Late 4th century || 
|-
| {{ill|Kani Sawako Kofun|ja|蟹沢古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kani Sawako Kofun|qq=}} (extinct)
|| {{ill|Takasaki|en|Takasaki|WD=Q336438|lt=Takasaki City|1=Takasaki}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}} || Circular (possibly square-shaped) || || Inscribed with a “{{ill|Zhengshi 1|ja|正始元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhengshi 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” inscription. Same-mold mirror as from the Old Mochida No. 48 Kofun.
|-
| {{ill|Akagi Tsukakizuka Kofun|ja|赤城塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagi Tsukakizuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (at Nishiyama Jinja Kofun)
|| {{ill|Itakura, Gunma|en|Itakura, Gunma|WD=Q1203771|lt=Itakura Town|1=Itakura, Gunma}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}} || Circular || 4th century || Triangular rim Buddhist beast mirror
|-
| 【Three Honoki Sozai Kofun】 || {{ill|Fujioka, Gunma|en|Fujioka, Gunma|WD=Q596486|lt=Fujioka City|1=Fujioka, Gunma}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}} || Uncertain || 4th century || Three triangular rim divine beast mirrors; possibly related to the Kanda Asama Shrine Kofun
|-
| {{ill|Tegozuka Kofun|ja|手古塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tegozuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (extinct)
|| {{ill|Kisarazu|en|Kisarazu|WD=Q335427|lt=Kisarazu City|1=Kisarazu}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}} || || || Imitation mirror; similar to the same-mold mirror from the Kawai Kofun and the Zōsan Kofun in Shimane Prefecture
|-
| {{ill|Jōyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori City)|ja|城山1号墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōyama No. 1 Kofun (Katori City)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
|| {{ill|Katori, Chiba|en|Katori, Chiba|WD=Q858357|lt=Katori City|1=Katori, Chiba}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}} || Keyhole-shaped kofun || Mid-6th century || Mirror excavated from a late-period kofun
|-

|-

|-

|-

|-

|-
| {{ill|Nakahachiman Kofun|ja|那珂八幡古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahachiman Kofun|qq=}}
|| {{ill|Fukuoka|en|Fukuoka|WD=Q26600|lt=Fukuoka City|1=Fukuoka}}, {{ill|Hakata-ku, Fukuoka|en|Hakata-ku, Fukuoka|WD=Q1330839|lt=Hakata Ward|1=Hakata-ku, Fukuoka}} || Keyhole-shaped kofun || Mid-3rd century || Triangular Rim, Five Gods Four Beasts Mirror
|-
| {{ill|Ishizukayama Kofun|en|Ishizukayama Kofun|WD=Q11585466|lt=Ishizukayama Kofun|1=Ishizukayama Kofun}}
|| {{ill|Miyako District, Fukuoka|en|Miyako District, Fukuoka|WD=Q1156359|lt=Kuwabara, Kyoto District|1=Miyako District, Fukuoka}}, {{ill|Kanda, Fukuoka|en|Kanda, Fukuoka|WD=Q1206876|lt=Karita Town|1=Kanda, Fukuoka}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}} || Keyhole-shaped kofun || Late 3rd century || Same-mold mirror as the Tsubinai Ōtsukayama Kofun; 7 faces recovered
|-
| {{ill|Ikisan Chōshizuka Kofun|en|Ikisan Chōshizuka Kofun|WD=Q48752705|lt=Ichikiyama Choushizuka Kofun|1=Ikisan Chōshizuka Kofun}}
|| {{ill|Itoshima, Fukuoka|en|Itoshima, Fukuoka|WD=Q1011145|lt=Itoshima City|1=Itoshima}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}} || Keyhole-shaped kofun || Late 4th century || 8 triangular rim divine beast mirrors recovered (claimed to be Japanese-made)<ref>{{sfn|古田武彦|2016|pp=262-263}}</ref>
|-
| {{ill|Ikinomiya Ichi-gō Kofun|ja|岩上祭祀遺構|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikinomiya Ichi-gō Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
|| {{ill|Munakata, Fukuoka|en|Munakata, Fukuoka|WD=Q819603|lt=Munakata City|1=Munakata, Fukuoka}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}} – part of Okinoshima || || Late 4th to 5th century || An ancient ritual site rather than a kofun
|-
| {{ill|Akatsukizuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akatsukizuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 川部・高森古墳群}}
|| {{ill|Usa, Ōita|en|Usa, Ōita|WD=Q114147|lt=Usa City|1=Usa, Ōita}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Ōita Prefecture|1=Ōita Prefecture}} – Usafudoki no Oka || Keyhole-shaped kofun || Late 3rd century || Part of the Kawabe–Takamori Kofun Group; 5 faces recovered
|-
| (Related to) Mochida Old No. 48 Kofun
|| {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}} – [Mochida area] || Circular kofun || || Part of the Mochida Kofun Group. In the early Showa period, extensive looting occurred.
|}

== Cultural Properties ==
=== Nara Prefecture Designated Cultural Property ===
* Historic Site
** {{ill|Sanryobo Kofun Group|ja|三陵墓古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanryobo Kofun Group|qq=}} – Designated on March 22, 1996 (Heisei 8).<ref name="一覧">{{PDFlink|[http://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/143530/kenshiitiran.pdf Nara Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties List]}} (Nara Prefecture Website).</ref>

{{Kofun navbar}}

{{translated page|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|version=103954791|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:三角縁神獣鏡]]
[[ja:三角縁神獣鏡が確認された主な古墳]]

<!--Triangular-rimmed Shinjukyo mirror--><!--Triangular-rimmed Shinjukyo mirror--><!--Triangular-rimmed Shinjukyo mirror--><!--Triangular-rimmed Shinjukyo mirror--><!-- (Due to the lack of valid sources for the number of excavations in Fukuoka Prefecture, this part has been omitted temporarily.) For example, over 40 mirrors have been found in Fukuoka Prefecture and over 28 in the Kibi region. This suggests that the Kinki region (Kyoto, Nara, Osaka) centered on Nara accounts for a significant number of finds. Furthermore, the number of excavations in the Kinki region exceeds 270, making up more than half of the total. --><!--|title=Sources on total number of mirrors and their distribution are unclear. See notes for details.|talksection=About the number of mirrors and their distribution|date=October 2018}} --><!--Triangular-rimmed Shinjukyo mirror--><!--Triangular-rimmed Shinjukyo mirror--><!--Triangular-rimmed Shinjukyo mirror--><!--Triangular-rimmed Shinjukyo mirror-->
<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->
